RAVI MEWFA
Ci IA I{MA N
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August 23,
1996
David P. Dillwood,
CPA
Zainer, Rinehart
& Clark
3510 Unocal Place #350
Santa Rosa,
CA
95403
Re:
Your Request for General
Assistance
Our File No.
G-96-235
Dear Mr.
Diliwood:
You have raised an issue concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
!
It appears that there
is some misunderstanding on your part concerning the role of the
Fair Political Practices Commission and the restrictions placed on
campaign credit card and charge accounts.
I hope the following
general information will prove helpful.
The Act was enacted by an initiative measure passed by the
voters in 1974.
It established the Fair Political Practices
Commission,
which has primary responsibility for the impartial,
effective administration and implementation of the Act.
(Section
83111.)
The Commission also is the agency with primary
enforcement authority over the provisions of the Act.
The Commission may adopt regulations,
issue legal opinions,
and provide written advice interpreting the provisions of the Act.
Contrary to the statement in your letter, it is the Secretary of
State and local filing officers who “review the reports to ensure
that the campaigns are indeed complying with the rules” and have
no other authority to create new regulations or otherwise
interpret the Act.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
I Siiii
Hi
SU(1
S
:\Ii\iu,
‘)‘i-3
()I()


David P.
Diliwood
Our File No.
G-96-235
Page
2
Regulation 18524
(copy enclosed)
was adopted by the
Commission in 1989 to assist candidates and their campaign
committees in complying with the bank account requirements added
to the Act by Proposition 73 in 1988.
Subsection
(c)
of
Regulation 18524 states:
The car-fldate may establish one or more cre’.it card
accounts or one or more charge accounts for the
campaign bank account.
Expenditures for payment
of charges incurred on each credit card or charge
account shall be made only from the campaign bank
account.
The credit card and charge accounts
shall be used only for expenses associated with
the candidate’s election to the specific elective
office designated in the statement of intention
or expenses associated with holding that office.
(Emphasis added.)
I trust this letter has adequately addressed your concerns.
Please contact me at
(916)
322-5660 if you have additional
questions.
Sincerely,
)
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
Enclosure

Dillwood, CPA Zainer, Rinehart & Clark 3510 Unocal Place #350 Santa Rosa, CA 95403 Re: Your Request for General Assistance Our File No. G-96-235 Dear Mr. Diliwood: You have raised an issue concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 ! It appears that there is some misunderstanding on your part concerning the role of the Fair Political Practices Commission and the restrictions placed on campaign credit card and charge accounts. I hope the following general information will prove helpful. The Act was enacted by an initiative measure passed by the voters in 1974. It established the Fair Political Practices Commission, which has primary responsibility for the impartial, effective administration and implementation of the Act. (Section 83111.) The Commission also is the agency with primary enforcement authority over the provisions of the Act. The Commission may adopt regulations, issue legal opinions, and provide written advice interpreting the provisions of the Act. Contrary to the statement in your letter, it is the Secretary of State and local filing officers who “review the reports to ensure that the campaigns are indeed complying with the rules” and have no other authority to create new regulations or otherwise interpret the Act. Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et g. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. Diliwood Our File No. G-96-235 Page 2 Regulation 18524 (copy enclosed) was adopted by the Commission in 1989 to assist candidates and their campaign committees in complying with the bank account requirements added to the Act by Proposition 73 in 1988. Subsection (c) of Regulation 18524 states: The car-fldate may establish one or more cre’.it card accounts or one or more charge accounts for the campaign bank account. Expenditures for payment of charges incurred on each credit card or charge account shall be made only from the campaign bank account. The credit card and charge accounts shall be used only for expenses associated with the candidate’s election to the specific elective office designated in the statement of intention or expenses associated with holding that office. (Emphasis added.) I trust this letter has adequately addressed your concerns. Please contact me at (916) 322-5660 if you have additional questions. Sincerely, ) Carla Wardlow Division Chief Technical Assistance Division Enclosure
